language.subtitle: Choose your preferred language for the interface
main.menu.info_body:
A desktop application for speed reading
and cognitive training.

15 exercises across three categories:
Perception • Cognition • Reading

16 training paths • 11 color profiles
Progress tracking • CSV/JSON export
Per-user profiles • Adjustable font scale
text.library.widget.reserved_name_msg: "{name}" is a built-in text. Choose a different name.
guide.flash.title: FLASH PERCEPTION
guide.flash.body:
HOW TO USE:
Begin with fixed digits (e.g., just '1 digit') to reduce anxiety. Once comfortable, move to 'Overlapping' sets (e.g., 2-4 digits) to stop your brain from predicting the length.

THE SCIENCE:
Tachistoscopic training (brief visual exposure) has been used since the 1800s to study perception. Research shows that the visual system can process information in as little as 13 milliseconds (Potter et al., MIT, 2014). With practice, the brain learns to encode more information per fixation by strengthening the ventral visual stream.

BENEFITS:
- Increases the number of characters recognized per eye fixation
- Trains iconic memory (ultra-short-term visual buffer)
- Reduces subvocalization by forcing faster-than-speech processing
- Improves digit span, a core measure of working memory capacity
guide.eyespan.title: EYE-SPAN EXPANSION
guide.eyespan.body:
HOW TO USE:
Horizontal: Trains width for reading lines.
Vertical: Trains 'Block Reading' (seeing multiple lines at once).
Mixed: Trains agility.
Focus only on the fixation cross (+) in the center.

THE SCIENCE:
The fovea (central 2 degrees of vision) provides sharp detail, but the parafovea (extending to ~5 degrees) can identify words during reading. Skilled readers extract useful information from a wider perceptual span: about 14-15 characters to the right of fixation in English (Rayner, 1998). Training peripheral recognition expands this effective span.

BENEFITS:
- Widens the perceptual span, reducing the number of fixations per line
- Trains parafoveal preview, allowing the brain to pre-process upcoming words
- Vertical mode develops block reading, useful for scanning documents
- Directly increases reading speed by covering more text per fixation
guide.schulte.title: SCHULTE GRID
guide.schulte.body:
HOW TO USE:
Find numbers 1 through 25 in order. Keep your gaze fixed on the center square and use peripheral vision to locate each number.
Score: +5 for correct click, -2 for wrong click.

THE SCIENCE:
Schulte tables were developed by German psychiatrist Walter Schulte to measure and train visual attention. Studies in sports psychology show that athletes who train with Schulte grids develop faster visual search times and broader attentional fields (Appelbaum & Erickson, 2018). The exercise engages both the dorsal attention network (spatial awareness) and the ventral attention network (target detection).

BENEFITS:
- Expands peripheral awareness without moving the eyes
- Improves visual search speed and selective attention
- Strengthens the ability to suppress distractors
- Used by pilots and athletes to sharpen situational awareness
guide.pacer.title: PACER FLOW
guide.pacer.body:
HOW TO USE:
Paste your reading material, set a target WPM, and follow the highlight. Do not regress (look back). Let the pacer pull your eyes forward.

THE SCIENCE:
Regression (re-reading) accounts for 10-15% of all eye movements during reading and is a major speed limiter (Rayner et al., 2012). A visual pacer acts as an external metronome that suppresses regression and gradually pushes reading rate above the comfort zone. This leverages the spacing effect: slightly exceeding your current speed forces deeper encoding.

BENEFITS:
- Eliminates regression, the single largest source of wasted reading time
- Trains a steady, rhythmic eye movement pattern
- Gradually increases comfortable reading speed through progressive overload
- Improves focus and reduces mind-wandering during reading
guide.priming.title: EYE PRIMING
guide.priming.body:
HOW TO USE:
Follow the dot with your eyes only. Keep your head still.
SACCADE modes: the dot jumps between positions in structured patterns (horizontal, vertical, diagonal, expanding). These train fast, accurate eye jumps — the primary movement used in reading.
PURSUIT modes: the dot moves continuously along a path (line, circle, figure-8). Track it smoothly without jumping ahead.

THE SCIENCE:
The eyes are controlled by six extraocular muscles per eye. Saccades and smooth pursuit engage different neural circuits: the frontal eye fields for voluntary saccades and the medial superior temporal area for smooth pursuit (Krauzlis, 2004). Training both systems improves oculomotor flexibility. Sports vision research shows that structured saccadic training improves reaction time and visual search speed (Appelbaum & Erickson, 2018).

BENEFITS:
- Warms up extraocular muscles to reduce eye strain during exercises
- Saccade patterns train the exact eye movements used in reading
- Smooth pursuit develops tracking accuracy and eye-hand coordination
- Expanding saccades progressively increase oculomotor range
- Reduces the risk of eye fatigue during extended training sessions
guide.rsvp.title: RSVP (RAPID SERIAL VISUAL PRESENTATION)
guide.rsvp.body:
HOW TO USE:
Paste your reading material or use the sample text. Set your target WPM and press Start. Words appear one at a time at the center of the screen. Keep your eyes fixed on the center — no eye movement needed.

THE SCIENCE:
RSVP eliminates the two biggest speed limiters in reading: saccadic eye movements and regression. By presenting words at a fixed point, the eyes stay still while the brain processes each word. Research by Forster (1970) established RSVP as a standard paradigm in psycholinguistics. At speeds above ~300 WPM, the brain is forced to abandon subvocalization (internal speech) and process text visually, building a direct pathway from visual input to comprehension.

BENEFITS:
- Eliminates eye movement overhead, isolating pure word processing speed
- Breaks the subvocalization habit by exceeding inner speech rate
- Trains rapid lexical access — recognizing words faster
- Builds comfort with higher reading speeds before applying them to normal text
- Measurable: track your comfortable WPM over time
guide.chunking.title: CHUNKING (PHRASE READING)
guide.chunking.body:
HOW TO USE:
Paste your text, choose how many words per chunk (2-6), set the display speed, and press Start. Word groups flash at the center of the screen. Read each group as a single unit of meaning.

THE SCIENCE:
Miller's Law (1956) established that working memory holds ~7 chunks of information. By grouping words into meaningful phrases, you effectively multiply the text processed per cognitive 'slot.' Skilled readers naturally fixate on word groups rather than individual words — their perceptual span covers 2-4 words per fixation (Rayner, 1998). Chunking training accelerates this transition from word-by-word to phrase-level reading.

BENEFITS:
- Trains the brain to process word groups as single units
- Directly reduces the number of fixations needed per line
- Builds the core skill that separates fast readers from slow ones
- Complements RSVP: RSVP trains speed, chunking trains breadth
- Improves reading comprehension by preserving phrase-level meaning
guide.sequence_memory.title: SEQUENCE MEMORY
guide.sequence_memory.body:
HOW TO USE:
A sequence of items (numbers, words, or both) flashes one at a time. After the last item, reproduce the sequence in the correct order by clicking the buttons. The sequence gets longer when you succeed and shorter when you fail (staircase method).

THE SCIENCE:
Working memory capacity is one of the strongest predictors of reading comprehension and general cognitive ability (Conway et al., 2005). Sequence recall tasks engage the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad — the two subsystems of Baddeley's working memory model. Training with increasing sequence lengths pushes the capacity boundary, and the staircase method keeps difficulty at the optimal challenge point where learning is most efficient (Ericsson, 2006).

BENEFITS:
- Expands working memory span for reading and learning
- Trains ordered recall, which supports text comprehension
- Number mode builds digit span (used in cognitive assessments)
- Word mode strengthens verbal working memory
- Mixed mode trains cognitive flexibility and dual coding
guide.rapid_decision.title: RAPID DECISION GRID
guide.rapid_decision.body:
HOW TO USE:
A grid of numbers appears. Follow the rule for the selected mode:
- Ascending: classic 1→2→3... order
- Descending: start from the highest number
- Even Only: click only even numbers in ascending order
- Odd Only: click only odd numbers in ascending order
- Alternating Colors: red numbers ascending, blue descending, alternate between them
- Timed modes add a countdown — finish before time runs out.

THE SCIENCE:
Adding rules to visual search tasks engages executive function and the prefrontal cortex alongside the attention networks used in standard Schulte grids. This dual demand — visual search plus rule application — mirrors real-world decision-making under time pressure (Diamond, 2013). Research on task-switching shows that practicing rule alternation improves cognitive flexibility and reduces switch costs.

BENEFITS:
- Trains decision speed under cognitive load
- Improves task-switching and cognitive flexibility
- Strengthens inhibitory control (ignoring irrelevant numbers)
- Builds on Schulte grid skills with higher-order demands
guide.peripheral_flash.title: PERIPHERAL FLASH
guide.peripheral_flash.body:
HOW TO USE:
Keep your eyes fixed on the center cross at all times. A stimulus (letter, number, or shape) will flash briefly at the edge of the screen. Identify what appeared without moving your eyes from the center.

THE SCIENCE:
Peripheral vision processes information through the magnocellular pathway, which is faster but lower resolution than central (foveal) vision. Training peripheral awareness expands the useful field of view (UFOV), a measure strongly correlated with reading speed, driving safety, and athletic performance (Ball et al., 1988). Professional athletes and esports players consistently show wider UFOV than untrained individuals.

BENEFITS:
- Expands useful field of view for faster reading
- Trains stimulus detection without eye movement
- Improves situational awareness for sports and driving
- Strengthens the magnocellular visual pathway
- Difficulty scales via flash duration and eccentricity
guide.mot.title: MULTIPLE OBJECT TRACKING
guide.mot.body:
HOW TO USE:
Several dots appear on screen. Some are highlighted as targets. Memorize which ones are targets, then all dots become identical and start moving. Track the targets with your attention (not your eyes). When movement stops, click the dots you believe were the targets. Selected dots turn highlighted — feedback is only shown after all selections are made: green = correct, orange = missed target, red = wrong pick.

THE SCIENCE:
Multiple Object Tracking (MOT) was introduced by Pylyshyn & Storm (1988) to study how the visual system tracks multiple moving objects simultaneously. NeuroTracker, a commercial MOT system, is used by professional sports teams (NFL, NHL, Premier League) and military organizations. Research shows MOT training transfers to improved decision-making in dynamic environments (Faubert & Sidebottom, 2012) and correlates with athletic performance.

BENEFITS:
- Trains sustained attention across multiple moving objects
- Improves dynamic visual acuity and spatial awareness
- Enhances performance in fast-paced sports and gaming
- Strengthens the dorsal attention network
- Difficulty scales via target count, speed, and duration
guide.split_attention.title: SPLIT ATTENTION
guide.split_attention.body:
HOW TO USE:
Two tasks happen each round. A word flashes in the center — remember it. At the same time (or just after), a colored shape appears at the screen edge. After both disappear you answer two questions: which word was shown, and which shape appeared.

Sequential mode shows the center word first, then the peripheral shape. Simultaneous mode shows both at once, demanding true divided attention.

THE SCIENCE:
Divided attention tasks measure the brain's ability to process multiple information streams in parallel. Neuroimaging studies (Corbetta & Shulman, 2002) show that the dorsal and ventral attention networks must coordinate when attention is split between central and peripheral targets. Training this coordination improves multitasking performance and reduces attentional bottleneck effects (Dux et al., 2009).

BENEFITS:
- Trains divided attention across central and peripheral vision
- Improves multitasking under time pressure
- Strengthens coordination between dorsal and ventral attention networks
- Transfers to real-world tasks like driving, sports, and monitoring
- Difficulty scales via flash duration and simultaneous vs. sequential mode
guide.reaction_time.title: REACTION TIME
guide.reaction_time.body:
HOW TO USE:
Simple: A green circle appears after a random delay. Click or press X as fast as you can. Responding before the stimulus appears counts as 'too early' and the round is repeated.

Choice: A colored shape appears — press the matching number key (1 = Green, 2 = Red, 3 = Blue, 4 = Yellow). Press X to skip a round. Speed and accuracy both matter.

Go/No-Go: Green circle = click or X (Go). Red circle = don't react (No-Go). Tests impulse control — false alarms on red count against you.

KEYBOARD:
X — respond (all modes)  |  1-4 — select color (choice mode)
Ctrl+Enter — start exercise from config screen

THE SCIENCE:
Simple reaction time averages 200-250ms in adults (Luce, 1986). Choice reaction time increases logarithmically with the number of alternatives (Hick's Law, 1952). Go/No-Go tasks engage the right inferior frontal gyrus for response inhibition (Aron et al., 2004). Regular training can reduce reaction times by 10-20% and improve inhibitory control.

BENEFITS:
- Measures and improves processing speed
- Choice mode trains rapid decision-making under uncertainty
- Go/No-Go strengthens impulse control and response inhibition
- Results use median RT (resistant to outliers)
- Transfers to driving, sports, and gaming performance
guide.slide_processing.title: SLIDE PROCESSING
guide.slide_processing.body:
HOW TO USE:
A text slide with facts, numbers, and details appears for a limited time. Read and absorb as much as you can before it disappears. Then answer 2-3 comprehension questions about specific details.

A countdown timer shows remaining display time. Focus on numbers, names, and key facts — questions target specific details, not general understanding.

THE SCIENCE:
Rapid information extraction is a core skill in speed reading. Research by Rayner & Castelhano (2007) shows that trained readers develop efficient scanning patterns that prioritize high-information regions. The ability to extract key facts from briefly presented material improves with practice and transfers to real-world tasks like skimming reports, processing dashboards, and studying.

BENEFITS:
- Trains rapid extraction of key information
- Improves scanning efficiency and prioritization
- Builds working memory for structured data
- Difficulty scales via display time and content density
- Transfers to report reading, studying, and data analysis
guide.spaced_repetition.title: SPACED REPETITION
guide.spaced_repetition.body:
HOW TO USE:
Select a deck and configure options on the right panel. A reading phase shows all cards (front and back) so you can familiarize yourself — skip it anytime with 'Skip to Review'. During review, recall the answer, then press X or click 'Show Answer' to reveal it. Rate your recall: Again (forgot), Hard, Good, or Easy. The system schedules the next review based on your rating.

OPTIONS:
Normal / Reverse — Reverse shows the back and asks you to recall the front.
Time Limit — Adds a countdown per card. The answer auto-reveals when time runs out.
Session Size — Limits how many cards per session.

KEYBOARD:
X — reveal answer  |  1-4 — rate (Again/Hard/Good/Easy)
Arrow keys or A/D — navigate reading phase

THE SCIENCE:
Spaced repetition exploits the spacing effect (Ebbinghaus, 1885): information is retained longer when reviews are spread over increasing intervals. The SM-2 algorithm (Wozniak, 1990) adapts intervals based on recall quality, keeping each card at the optimal review point just before forgetting. This is the most efficient known method for long-term memorization, used by medical students, language learners, and competitive memory athletes.

BENEFITS:
- Maximizes retention with minimal review time
- Adapts to individual learning speed per card
- Builds durable long-term memory
- 10 built-in decks across vocabulary, science, history, and more
- Create custom decks for any subject
- Streak tracking rewards consistent recall
intro.section.0.title: WHAT IS NEURAL SPEED ACADEMY?
intro.section.0.body:
Your brain processes far more information than you consciously use. Neural Speed Academy trains the visual, cognitive, and reading skills that unlock that capacity — through 15 targeted exercises, 16 structured training paths, and detailed progress tracking.

Every exercise produces measurable scores. Every session is logged. Whether you train alone, in a classroom, or in a clinical setting, your progress is visible and exportable.
intro.section.1.title: WHO IS THIS FOR?
intro.section.1.body:
• Students — Read textbooks faster, retain more for exams, and process lecture material efficiently. Spaced repetition and comprehension exercises directly support study workflows.

• Professionals — Handle information overload. Scan reports, process emails, and extract key facts faster. Decision speed and divided attention exercises transfer to high-pressure work.

• ADHD / Autism — Structured focus training with clear goals and immediate feedback. Visual exercises build sustained attention without relying on text-heavy tasks. 9 color profiles reduce sensory overload.

• Seniors — Maintain cognitive sharpness. Reaction time, working memory, and attention exercises are among the most evidence-backed interventions for healthy cognitive aging.

• Athletes — Peripheral vision, object tracking, and rapid decision-making directly improve field awareness and split-second reactions in competitive sports.

• Clinicians & Researchers — Measurable baselines, per-user profiles, session history, and CSV/JSON export provide the data needed for clinical assessment and research protocols.
intro.fact.0: Reaction time is one of the strongest predictors of cognitive health in aging research. A 2014 meta-analysis found that computerized cognitive training improved processing speed by 0.31 standard deviations in older adults (Lampit et al., PLOS Medicine).
intro.section.2.title: PERCEPTION — 5 EXERCISES
intro.section.2.body:
Perception exercises train the raw speed and width of your visual intake — how much you see in a single glance.

• Flash Numbers — Digits appear for a fraction of a second. You recall them. This trains iconic memory — the brief visual buffer that determines how much you capture per eye fixation. The same skill that lets a chess master see a board position in one glance.

• Word Drills — Whole words flashed at increasing speed. Builds instant word recognition so reading becomes pattern matching rather than letter-by-letter decoding.

• Eye-Span — Two items appear at the edges of your vision. You identify both. This widens your perceptual span — trained readers fixate 2–3 times per line instead of 5–7.

• Schulte Grid — Find numbers 1–25 in sequence on a grid without moving your eyes from center. Expands peripheral awareness — the same skill that lets you scan a page, notice a car approaching from the side, or read a dashboard at a glance.

• Peripheral Flash — Stimuli appear briefly in your side vision. Strengthens the visual field that most people underuse. Athletes, pilots, and drivers rely on this skill constantly.
intro.fact.1: Your peripheral vision covers over 180° of your visual field but most people only actively use the central 2–5° for reading. Training peripheral awareness can reduce the number of eye fixations per line by 40–60%.
intro.section.3.title: COGNITION — 5 EXERCISES
intro.section.3.body:
Cognition exercises train the processing layer — how fast you think, decide, and hold information in mind.

• Sequence Memory — Reproduce increasingly long sequences of numbers, letters, or colors. Working memory capacity predicts academic performance, problem-solving ability, and reading comprehension. This is one of the most researched cognitive training tasks.

• Rapid Decision — Find targets on a grid under time pressure. Trains the speed-accuracy tradeoff that governs real-world decisions — from choosing the right lane in traffic to selecting the right answer under exam pressure.

• Object Tracking (MOT) — Track 3–5 moving dots among distractors. This is the gold standard for divided attention research. Used by professional sports teams (NHL, Premier League) to train field awareness.

• Split Attention — Process a word task and a shape task simultaneously. Real life rarely presents one thing at a time — this exercise trains the ability to handle parallel demands.

• Reaction Time — Simple, choice, and go/no-go modes measure and improve response speed. Go/no-go specifically trains impulse control — the ability to withhold a response when needed.
intro.fact.2: Working memory training can transfer to fluid intelligence. Jaeggi et al. (2008, PNAS) showed that dual n-back training improved reasoning ability in proportion to training duration. Sequence Memory exercises target the same underlying capacity.
intro.section.4.title: READING — 5 EXERCISES
intro.section.4.body:
Reading exercises target the four bottlenecks that limit reading speed: too many fixations, regression (re-reading), subvocalization (inner voice), and narrow attention span.

• Pacer & Quiz — Text scrolls at a set WPM, forcing forward momentum. Eliminates regression — the habit of re-reading that wastes 10–15% of reading time. Comprehension quiz afterward ensures speed doesn't sacrifice understanding.

• RSVP Reader — One word at a time, faster than you can speak. This breaks the subvocalization barrier — the inner voice that limits most readers to ~150 WPM. Forces direct visual-to-meaning processing.

• Chunking — Text is highlighted in 2–7 word groups. Trains your eyes to process phrases as single units instead of individual words. The same skill that lets experienced readers absorb a sentence in 2–3 fixations.

• Spaced Repetition — The SM-2 algorithm schedules reviews at optimal intervals for long-term retention. Used by medical students, language learners, and anyone who needs to remember large amounts of information.

• Slide Processing — Read a passage for a limited time, then answer questions from memory. Trains active reading — the habit of extracting key information on the first pass.
intro.fact.3: An average reader processes 200–250 words per minute. Research shows that 4 weeks of consistent training typically produces 50–100% speed gains while maintaining comprehension. The key is daily practice, not marathon sessions.
intro.section.5.title: EYE WARMUP
intro.section.5.body:
The Eye Warmup (Priming) exercise guides your eyes through smooth pursuit, saccade, and convergence patterns. Use it before training sessions to prepare the extraocular muscles and reduce eye strain.

Think of it as stretching before a workout. 60–90 seconds of eye priming improves tracking accuracy and reduces fatigue during longer training sessions.
intro.section.6.title: TRAINING PATHS
intro.section.6.body:
16 structured paths organize exercises into progressive sessions so you don't have to plan your own training:

• Speed Reading — Daily Warmup, Foundation, and paths targeting 300 / 400 / 600 / 800 WPM. Each path builds on the previous one with increasing difficulty.
• Cognitive — Perception Master, Memory Foundations, Attention & Focus, Mental Agility.
• Visual — Peripheral Vision, Visual Tracking.
• Comprehension — Rapid Comprehension, Study Session.
• Mixed — Full Brain Workout, Quick Cognitive Check.

Paths track your progress step by step. Pick up where you left off, or restart anytime.
intro.section.7.title: TRACKING & ANALYTICS
intro.section.7.body:
Every session is recorded automatically:

• XP and leveling system for motivation and progress.
• Personal bests with exercise-appropriate metrics (accuracy, time, WPM, reaction time).
• Training consistency calendar — see your active days at a glance.
• Per-exercise progress charts with trend lines.
• Tiered insights — immediate session feedback, 7-day trends, and long-term strength/weakness analysis.
• Full session history with CSV and JSON export for research, clinical reports, or personal records.
intro.section.8.title: ACCESSIBILITY
intro.section.8.body:
Neural Speed Academy is designed to be usable by everyone:

• 9 color profiles including high contrast, warm focus, and monochrome options for visual sensitivities.
• Adjustable font scale (80–150%) for comfortable reading.
• Per-user preferences — each profile remembers its own theme and font size.
• Keyboard navigation for all core functions.
• Clean, distraction-free interface designed for focus.
intro.fact.4: Divided attention improves with practice even in adults over 60. A 2013 study (Anguera et al., Nature) showed that multitasking training in older adults produced gains that transferred to sustained attention and working memory.
intro.section.9.title: REALISTIC EXPECTATIONS
intro.section.9.body:
Research supports that consistent training (15–20 min/day, 4–5 days/week) produces measurable gains within 2–4 weeks.

• Reading speed: 50–100% improvement is typical.
• Working memory: gains appear after 2–3 weeks of daily practice.
• Reaction time: measurable improvement within days.
• Peripheral awareness: noticeable expansion within 1–2 weeks.

The key is consistency, not intensity. Short daily sessions outperform occasional long ones.

Claims of 1000+ WPM with full comprehension are not supported by peer-reviewed research. This program focuses on achievable, evidence-backed improvements.
intro.section.10.title: LIMITATIONS — WHAT THIS APP CANNOT DO
intro.section.10.body:
Neural Speed Academy trains component skills in isolation. That training only becomes useful when you apply it to real tasks. Important things to understand:

• Exercises are not a substitute for reading. Improving your RSVP speed or peripheral span means nothing if you don't practice reading actual books, articles, and documents.

• Speed without comprehension is not reading. If you can't recall or explain what you read, you were scanning, not reading. Always test yourself.

• Transfer is not automatic. Research shows that cognitive training gains are strongest on tasks similar to the training. Broader transfer (e.g., from Sequence Memory to exam performance) requires deliberate effort to apply the skills.

• No app replaces professional help. If you have a diagnosed reading disability, ADHD, or visual processing disorder, this app can supplement — but not replace — guidance from a qualified specialist.
intro.fact.5: A 2016 review (Simons et al., Psychological Science in the Public Interest) found that cognitive training reliably improves performance on trained tasks, but evidence for broad transfer to everyday abilities remains limited. The most effective approach combines targeted training with real-world practice.
intro.section.11.title: HOW TO GET THE MOST OUT OF YOUR TRAINING
intro.section.11.body:
Combine app exercises with deliberate real-world practice:

• Train 15–20 minutes, then read for 20+ minutes. Apply what you practiced — wider fixations, fewer regressions, chunking phrases.

• Use active reading strategies. Before reading, skim headings and structure. While reading, ask yourself questions. After reading, summarize from memory.

• Take notes. Mind maps, margin annotations, or brief summaries force you to process what you read. Speed without retention is wasted effort.

• Vary your material. Practice with textbooks, news articles, fiction, and technical documents. Each demands different reading strategies.

• Track your real-world progress. Time yourself reading a chapter, then test recall. Your app scores should correlate with real improvements — if they don't, adjust your approach.
stats.active_days: {count} active days in the last 12 weeks ({pct}%)
stats.export_desc: Export session history with exercise parameters, scores, and timing data. CSV is Excel-compatible; JSON is for programmatic analysis in R, SPSS, or Python.
stats.active_days_year: {count} active days in the last year ({pct}%)
intro.copy_tooltip: Copy introduction text to clipboard for use in training exercises
